exemplar.title: Which mathematical function (quadratic, cubic or piecewise) best models the first drop of the Hyperion roller coaster at Energylandia, and how can mathematical analysis enhance the understanding of its dynamics?

detailedExemplarData.title: Which mathematical function (quadratic, cubic or piecewise) best models the first drop of the Hyperion roller coaster at Energylandia, and how can mathematical analysis enhance the understanding of its dynamics?

subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear, logical narrative that builds from rationale through modelling, analysis, and conclusion
Well-defined headings (Rationale, Introduction, Modelling, Derivative Analysis, Arc Length, Conclusion, Limitations) that structure the report
Relevant tables and graphs placed near the corresponding discussion to support the text
weaknesses: Minor digressions (personal reflections mid-analysis) interrupt the technical flow
Some unnecessary repetition of aims and lengthy quotations of URLs
Occasional inconsistencies in referencing style and image placement
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and shows clear logical development with a recognisable structure and mostly relevant content, aligning with the band 3 descriptor for Presentation. Minor irrelevancies and formatting inconsistencies prevent it from reaching band 4.

strengths: Consistent use of standard function notation, derivatives and piecewise definitions
Effective use of multiple representations: data tables, regression outputs, GeoGebra screenshots and algebraic expressions
Generally clear and logically sign-posted explanations that guide the reader through each step
weaknesses: Isolated notation slips (mixing f(x) and h(x), omitted multiplication signs)
Some axes in graphs are unlabeled and images appear pixelated
Minor rounding inconsistencies and occasional ambiguity in variable definitions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language and notation are mostly appropriate, representations are effective (though not sophisticated), and communication is clear with some inconsistencies, fitting the band 3 descriptor for Mathematical Communication.

strengths: Data extraction from an online photograph and choice of piecewise modelling demonstrate independent thinking
Personal rationale clearly links the topic to the studentâs interests in roller-coasters and architecture
Creativity shown in applying an SSE check and choosing transition points for the model
weaknesses: Mathematical techniques remain standard SL methods without novel development
Personal reflections occasionally interrupt the technical narrative
Initiatives, while significant, stop short of the originality required for the highest band
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is significant evidence of independent thinking, a clear personal approach, and noteworthy creativity, but it does not reach outstanding levels. This aligns with band 2 for Personal Engagement.

strengths: Meaningful interpretation of RÂ², derivatives and arc-length in context of roller-coaster design
Acknowledgement and contextualisation of limitations (data points, smoothness, rounding)
Explicit connections drawn between mathematical results and personal understanding of design and safety
weaknesses: Reflections are primarily descriptive and lack deeper critical analysis
Proposals for extensions are cursory rather than fully developed
Some repetition of methodology reduces conciseness in the reflective sections
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates meaningful reflection with some critique and clear connections to understanding, but lacks the depth and sustained critical analysis for band 3. It therefore meets the band 2 descriptor for Reflection.

strengths: Appropriate use of SL-level mathematics: quadratic, cubic and piecewise functions, derivatives, regression and arc-length
Effective interpretation of SSE and concavity to support model validity
Correct set-up of derivatives and integrals with plausible numerical results
weaknesses: Minor algebraic and notational errors (omitted coefficients, inconsistent derivative expressions)
Rounding inconsistencies and lack of justification for certain methodological choices (e.g., evaluation points)
Insufficient rigour in demonstrating continuity and differentiability at segment boundaries
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematics is mostly correct with relevant course-level applications and demonstrated understanding in parts, but lacks the precision and rigour for the higher bands. This performance corresponds to band 3 in Use of Mathematics.

strandSelections.reasoning: Selecting, scaling and extracting data from an online photograph, deciding to split the data set and construct a piecewise model, and performing derivative and arc-length analyses all demonstrate noticeable independent thinking beyond a template approach, but the mathematics itself remains standard SL techniques, so the evidence is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ.

The student shows understanding of regression, why piecewise modelling is preferable, and how derivatives/arc length relate to the physical situation, yet explanations occasionally blur statistical and mathematical reasoning and lack full justification of assumptions.  Overall, good understanding is demonstrated in parts, consistent with band 3.

Algebraic work is generally correct; derivative formulas and arc-length integrals are set up properly and numerical answers are plausible.  Minor errors/omissions exist (derivative of chosen quadratic uses earlier coefficients; rounding inconsistencies) but they do not substantially affect outcomes.  This matches âmathematics is mostly correctâ (band 3).

Most paragraphs address the central modelling task, yet the student repeatedly restates aims or personal feelings and includes lengthy quotations of URLs.  This results in noticeable but not overwhelming irrelevance and repetition, matching âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ.  Consequently the strand fits band 2 for conciseness.

Standard function notation, derivatives (hâ², hâ³), inequality symbols and piecewise notation are used correctly most of the time.  Isolated slips (mixing f(x) and h(x), omissions of Ã signs, inconsistent sub-script in RÂ²) are present but do not impede understanding.  Hence the language is âmostly appropriateâ rather than fully consistent.

Explanations are generally clear; steps are numbered or sign-posted, and conclusions (e.g. âthe track is falling because hâ²<0â) logically follow calculations.  Some ambiguity remains (confusing two quadratic models, minor rounding inconsistencies) so full consistency is not met; the descriptor for band 3 is satisfied.

Quadratic, cubic, piecewise functions, first/second derivatives, regression, SSE and arc-length are all at SL level and applied appropriately to the context.  Combining models into a continuous piecewise function and using the mathematics to draw design conclusions constitutes effective use, but the investigation stops short of sophisticated or rigorous techniques such as optimisation, so band 4 is most appropriate.

The exploration employs tables of data, multiple GeoGebra screenshots, calculator regression screens and algebraic expressions.  These complementary representations help the reader judge model fit.  However, axes are sometimes unlabeled and images pixelate, so the use is effective but not âsophisticatedâ, placing it in band 3.

The student comments on the meaning of RÂ² values, interprets derivatives in terms of rising/falling track, and links arc length to material cost and rider safety.  These constitute meaningful reflections, yet they remain descriptive and surface-level, lacking extended critique or probing questions, fitting band 2.

Employing a photograph to collect primary data, switching to piecewise modelling, and adding an SSE check show creativity and initiative beyond minimal requirements.  The techniques, however, remain conventional and there is no novel mathematical development, so the strand aligns with âsignificant creativity and initiativeâ (band 2).

The report follows a clear narrative from rationale, through modelling, analysis and conclusion.  Each step builds logically on the previous one; the reader can follow why quadratic->cubic->piecewise was attempted and how derivative/arc-length analyses follow from the final model.  Minor digressions (e.g. personal reflections mid-analysis) interrupt the flow but do not break coherence, so the work meets the descriptor for âcoherent and shows clear logical development,â though not the âexcellentâ standard demanded for band 4.

Limitations (few data points, assumption of smoothness, rounding errors) are acknowledged and contextualised.  The SSE check is used to validate model choice.  While this shows some critique, it is not deep or sustained; hence the strand meets âmeaningful analysis with some critiqueâ (band 2).

Reflections explicitly connect mathematical results to the studentâs understanding of roller-coaster design and personal perception of mathematics.  These links enhance understanding but are not explored in great depth, so the work satisfies band 2 rather than the strongest descriptor.

Headings (Rationale, Introduction, Modelling, Derivative Analysis, Arc Length, Conclusion, Limitations) give a recognisable introduction-body-conclusion structure, and tables/graphs are placed close to the relevant discussion.  Section ordering is sensible, but some images split awkwardly across pages and referencing is occasionally embedded in the text rather than footnoted consistently, so the organisation is âwell organisedâ rather than âexceptionally well organised.â

The topic is linked to the studentâs personal interest in roller-coasters and architecture, and personal decisions (choice of transition point, reflections on insecurity, SSE check) guide the investigation.  This constitutes a clear personal approach that drives the work, but it does not reach the depth or originality required for the highest band.

annotations.comment: The quadratic formula is introduced without linking its typical domain of applicabilityâexplain why this function is appropriate for modelling the entire drop initially.
The high RÂ² â 0.908 is well-interpreted, demonstrating an appropriate understanding of regression fit.
Graph 3 would benefit from clearly labeled axes and units to reinforce mathematical communication.
The cubic function explanation is clear, but define each coefficient (d,e,f,z) consistently when moving between f and g notation.
The reported regression output omits the coefficient f for the cubic modelâinclude all parameters to fully justify the model fit.
Including the emotional comment on insecurity interrupts the technical narrativeâkeep personal reflections in a separate reflection section.
The research question is clearly stated and aligns well with the explorationâs aim, providing a focused direction for modelling Hyperionâs drop.
The session and page-count block is administrative and distracts from contentâconsider removing it to maintain conciseness.
The personal rationale effectively shows genuine interest and links the topic to the studentâs background, showcasing clear personal engagement.
The rationale paragraph is very longâconsider breaking it into smaller paragraphs to improve readability and focus.
The methodology for extracting coordinates from the image mentions GeoGebra but lacks precise stepsâadd details on axis calibration and point-selection criteria.
The GeoGebra screenshot (Figure 1) lacks axis labels and scale markings, making it hard to judge precisionâinclude labeled axes.
The discussion of inconsistent Îx is insightful, but explain how non-uniform spacing might affect regression accuracy.
In Table 1, the x-coordinate of point A is labelled Î¸âthis is confusing; replace Î¸ with a numeric value or a consistent label.
The explanation of rounding to integers is lengthyâcondense this to focus on its impact on model accuracy.
Graph 1 visually represents the scatter plot clearly, helping to assess distribution of data points before modelling.
Clarify why the initial quadratic modelâs domain spans 0 â¤ x â¤ 42 before you later split the intervalâthis will aid reader comprehension.
Segmenting the data into two quadratics is an insightful approach that shows independent thinking in identifying model limitations.
In the quadratic formula for the second group, the term ââ 3.21 + 76.9â omits the x multiplierâcorrect to ââ3.21x + 76.9â for accuracy.
To ensure smoothness, explicitly solve and show the equality of h and hâ² at x = 20; this will confirm continuity and differentiability.
Specify which data points were used in the SSE calculation to allow reproducibility of the error analysis.
The inclusion of SSE = 1.79 reinforces the reliability of the piecewise approach by quantifying model accuracy.
The piecewise definition is well-presented in a single equation, clearly showing domain restrictions for each segment.
Graph 5 successfully visualizes the combined piecewise model alongside the data, confirming an excellent fit.
The conclusion describes the fit well but lacks deeper reflection on modelling assumptionsâlink back to practical constraints like safety.
The derivative formula uses 3Ã(â¦) notationâexpress hâ²(x)=â0.0369xÂ²+0.28xâ0.665 for clearer mathematical communication.
Selecting x=10 for derivative evaluation is arbitraryâjustify this choice or evaluate at a critical point for greater insight.
The concavity interpretation shows good understanding of second derivatives and their link to physical curvature.
The arc length explanation is denseâsplit into separate paragraphs for setup, physical interpretation, and calculation steps.
State the numerical integration method and tolerance when reporting Lâ63.6 m and Lâ31.8 m to substantiate accuracy of arc-length results.
The reflective summary is comprehensive, but it could be more conciseâfocus on key takeaways rather than repeating methodology.
The limitations are candidly discussed; to deepen critique, propose specific advanced functions (e.g., splines) to capture non-smooth curvature.
The self-assessment of strengths is clear and demonstrates an understanding of the studyâs rigour and relevance.
A proposed extension to examine alternative optimisation of drop shape with calculus-based techniques would further enhance personal initiative.
